title: The Rap-Up: Whale Raps, Dave East Over A Gap Band Flip + New Chris Crack
author: Steven Louis

full_text:
Screenshot via Dave East & Scram Jones – “Too Outlandish”/YouTube

The Rap-Up is the only weekly round-up providing you with the best rap songs you need to hear. Support real, independent music journalism by subscribing to Passion of the Weiss on Patreon.

Steven Louis is editor emeritus at Pets.com.

Rich The Factor – Orca Alley and various whale raps

VIDEO

Do you know what’s good with whales? For the uninitiated, check it out — so sick, right? I’d of course prefer you to stay here on The Rap-Up, but I’ll understand if your mind is melting and you now must go look at more whale photos. For those of us that remain, it’s time to bump Rich The Factor’s latest work.

It won’t be the latest for long. Rich The Factor released seven albums in 2025 and eight in 2024. Of course, several projects had the marine mammal motif. The North Face Whale (Vol. 3) opened with a warm, soulful intro; The Whale Museum closed out on a great Messy Marv feature. The Kansas City grinder has already teased his next drop with AI cover art of a whale dunking a basketball. But his February effort, Orca Alley, deserves full rotation.

“Long Nights” is rubbery and spacious, an understated highway mission that swerves into gray trance. “Swope Park” flickers the neon lights to celebrate K.C. and appreciate socially-distanced strippers. The one that’s really lodged in my head is “Whale Raps,” fastened atop skittering blues by Tank 816 and White Boy Made This. The hustler’s prayer now includes a psalm for Patrick Mahomes’ knee. Mac Dre’s spirit gets summoned from the import rig. Despite his feverish pace, Rich The Factor spits with a slow crackle, each line landing in thickness and then languidly dissolving. Somewhere, a beluga rubs its fins together and gets back on the paper trail. Do not use AI to make that image. I recommend Orca Alley to anyone in need of a second wind or a new No. 1 sea creature.

Dave East & Scram Jones – “Too Outlandish” (feat. King Beamo & Un Kasa)

VIDEO

Introducing … Dave East as Uncle Charlie! Your skepticism is warranted, but this Gap Band flip is pure serotonin. The proceedings start with Brooklyn driller King Beamo, whose flow bucks and buckles with its own rhythm. He owns the sample from the jump with “I’m too outstanding, too outlandish, came up scammin’.” Up next is Dipset disciple Un Kasa, who does so very much with his compressed verse. We meet the twins Tanisha and Dolores. We watch the product “metamorphis” [sic]. As he tells it, his Porsche has vomit-colored trim and seats the tone of dog poop. Dave East wraps up with tight handles and a four-syllable scheme. It’s a proven formula for Harlem hits — R&B record with the slightest beat boost, the “uptown, downtown” pump-up intro, New York gangster classicism over golden love croons — and I’m more than fine with someone else reclaiming it.

Chris Crack – “My Brother Knew the Real Angelo Roberts”

VIDEO

Just reading off a list of Chris Crack album titles would be funnier and more provocative than 99 percent of working standups. Battery Operated Simps … Free Sex … White People Think God Left Them in Charge. Yeah, that’ll play! Don’t get it twisted, though, he’s still an elite rapper with deceptive, soul-spilling punch. Here, the Chicago-born artist refers to Chief Lo — the 4 Corner Hustlers renegade that allegedly tried to blow up a West Side police precinct before his grisly murder in 1995. Chris Crack gets nostalgic with a lift from producer blckwndr, who sources warm winds off frozen Lake Michigan. “I watched my brother’s every move, so I was down to call it / they ain’t want me in the system, so was quick to try and stall me” turns the hook to a hymnal. While the squares dreamed of being cops and firefighters, our protagonist was flicking Bics in his room and knotting cigar guts. He also makes a clear-eyed pitch for a hustler’s pension. Maybe he’s destined to be the rap game Marvin Miller. The punchline is that there’s no punchline. It’s just music to scheme to, with an (alleged) rocket launcher beside the fireplace.

KP SKYWALKA – “Pistol Da Myers”

I’m fascinated by DC’s rising star KP Skywalka, who seems to rap diagonally through production. He pushes words straight into the downbeat, a slicing style befitting the giant sword tattooed across his neck. The delivery is twangy and the output is prolific. Skywalka just put out his first EP of 2026, and I’m stuck on “Pistol Da Myers.” It’s a quick, eerie glide. More importantly, it bends English syntax in a completely new fashion. In his jolting flow, “I’m Michael the Myers” sounds too natural to have ever been incorrect. He says “a juice of the green” twice, and it feels essential to the rhythm both times. No one is riding for the Universal Grammar guy anymore, so let’s just let KP lead the next generation.

We rely on your support to keep POW alive. Please take a second to donate on Patreon!
